# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. I want to get stunned by the plot twist of the movie.

**Score:** 84
**Body:** I got bored of the plot twist that some boring movies offer us , i am scrolling through Netflix but i couldn’t find any good movie with a better story and plot twist. Can anyone suggest me a movie ?   
**If its not in Netflix let me know in which ott platform its available.**  

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Vanishing 1988

Shutter Island 2010
  - The Vanishing is a great film
2. Primal Fear with Edward Norton. Crazy twist!
3. Parasite. If anyone out there has never seen it, you don't know what you're missing. It was very popular. 
4. Arlington Road (1999)

The Game (1997)

Upgrade (2018)


5. Trouble is, we tell you one you know there’s a twist.  
The best part of one is not knowing it’s coming.
6. Oldboy (2003)
7. Triangle
8. The first Saw movie 
9. Frailty (2001)

## 2. Looking for hidden Horror/Sci-Fi gems that legit don't get mentioned a ton. (Or non-genre movies, I'm desperate, lol.)

**Score:** 29
**Body:** I have not seen every movie ever, *obviously*, but sometimes it feels that way. I have seen A LOT, though. This is one of those times it feels like I've seen everything, as I can't seem to find anything to watch.

First, it was watching the all-timer lists where you see movies that show up on GOAT lists and movies everyone knows like The Shining, or Annihilation, or Arrival, or Halloween. 
I search and I search, and I read Reddit posts on surprise hits and then start getting into lesser known movies like Behind the Mask: The Rise of Leslie Vernon, and Aniara, and The Signal, and The Ritual. Etc, etc, etc.

Pretty soon, it feels like I'm getting to recommendations that are like, "Well it got 24% on RT from fans AND critics, but it's good, really!" or "Half the cast can't act, and the writing is terrible, but there are two minutes in the middle of the movie that are amazing!" type of recommendations.

Surely, there are more movies to be found out there, that for whatever reason can't seem to find. So what are your hidden gems and/or movies that you don't see on tons of lists, including some hidden gems lists, lol.

Excited to see what people come up with!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Strange Days
2. Aniara (2018) - space cruise goes wrong.   
The Ugly Stepsister (2025) - cinderella from a different perspective. Body horror.   
Tumbbad (2018) - Indian folklore story, very creepy 

My recent favourites that don't get mentioned often.
  - I absolutely loved Aniara
3. Triangle

Coherence

Event Horizon

Beyond The Black Rainbow

The Endless
4. Color Out of Space
5. Sphere - has a terrible rating (12% RT) but it is a special kind of nightmare sci fi for me

Mothman Prophecies - 52% RT but I love it as a weird supernatural horror with my favorite cryptid
6. Have you seen 30 Days of Night?
7. A Boy and His Dog. Very weird. Based on short story by Harlan Ellison 
8. Cube
9. A horror sci-fi that comes to mind is Life (2017) 

## 3. Movies about the slow erosion of self and the ultimate relief of existential unmasking

**Score:** 27
**Body:** I'm looking for deep, character-driven films about characters who find themselves suffocating in structures, environments, or expectations they don't truly belong in.

Specifically, films that showcase the destabilizing realization that you've been living a lie, followed by the powerful moment where the mask is finally dropped to reveal their true individual self.

What are the most powerful films you've seen that capture this exact theme of finding freedom outside of your raised framework?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Truman Show
  - Perfect answer
2. Synecdoche, New York
3. Falling Down
4. I feel like The Station Agent with Peter Dinklage might fit this request.  It was his first big movie role.
5. I think Swiss Army Man might work here. 

It’s an existential crisis wrapped up in delusion while trying to come to grips with the life you’ve made for yourself and its inevitable end.

Or it’s about a dude riding a gaseous corpse off a deserted island. Depends on your mood 🤣
6. Tom Wilkinson's performance in Michael Clayton is one of the most extraordinary performances that matches this description Ive ever seen. Clooneys character can be said to be going through this as well but Wilkinson is unforgettable.
7. Melancholia
8. I heart huckabees is exactly this
9. Toy Story, specifically Buzz Lightyear’s story arc. 

## 4. Recommend me movies that don't look "digitally bland."

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Is it just me or you also feel that a lot of modern movies feel visually "tasteless." The color grading often leans muted, shots change every few seconds, and I rarely come away remembering individual frames the way I do with older films.

I miss rich, expressive color palettes, cinematography that feels deliberate, and long takes that let a scene breathe instead of cutting away immediately. Not every movie needs all three, but it feels like those qualities are becoming less common.

I'd love recommendations for movies that excel in any of those areas. Whether it's unforgettable cinematography, incredible use of color, or long takes that completely immerse you in the scene.

Any language, country, or genre is welcome.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Birdcage - wonderful color palette, great lighting and so many long takes.
2. The Secret Life of Walter Mitty 
3. *Conclave* does wonders with the color palettes of the Vatican.
4. What Dreams May Come. The scene with the world made of paint is gorgeous! 
5. Train dreams
6. Most recently Bugonia blew me away with the cinematography & use of color. 
7. Troy 👍
8. Phonecian Scheme

The Secret Agent

No Other Choice

Decision to Leave
9. Moulin Rouge (2001)

Hugo (2011)

The Great Gatsby (2013)

Under the Tuscan Sun (2003)

It’s a Mad Mad Mad Mad world (1963)

What Dreams May Come (1998) 
10. Shark Boy and Lava Girl

## 5. Non-romantic feel good movies

**Score:** 23
**Body:** Hi! My brother just got broken up with from his 4 year relationship and is absolutely heartbroken. I’m about to visit him for a few days. What are some movies that we can enjoy together that don’t have romance as the central focus?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Project Hail Mary, The Full Monty, The Sheep Detectives, Harvey,
  - PHM for sure!! I think it’s free on Prime now!
    - I’ve seen it 4 times I think I might make it a 5th with him
  - *Harvey* is nearly perfect that way ☺️
2. School of Rock
3. You are a great sibling! 

Step Brothers  
Super Troopers   
Major League   
The Sandlot  
Night of the Museum   
Hook  
Finding Nemo (remind him to just keep swimming) 
4. Chef (2014)
5. Up
  - Might sting watching the beginning.  Maybe.
6. Secondhand Lions is one of my favorite comfort flicks. Also Wishful Drinking- Carrie Fisher's one woman show does a great job of taking you into a different world and laughing, and Love is Evol from Christopher Titus if he starts coming around to the recovery stage to understand that some relationships need to end and there will be a life beyond them.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Curry Barker absolutely nailed Ian's characterization in Obsession.

**Score:** 1137
**Body:** It's one of the most realistic portrayals of a fake friend, someone who seems to care about you on the surface but quietly tries to sabotage you at every turn while acting kind, supportive, and well-intentioned. The manipulation and constant maneuvering felt disturbingly believable.

What's your opinion of Ian? Do you see him as a villain in Obsession?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. There's a line in the script that I wish they'd kept in the final cut that maybe shows Bear and Ian are very similar. When Bear drives Nikki home from trivia, she asks why Ian forces everyone to drink so much.

**NICKY: Why does Ian force everyone to drink so much?**

_A beat. Bear thinks about it._

**BEAR: Maybe... maybe he thinks if he can force people to do things, they'll discover it's what they've always wanted.**

It's such a small moment, but I think it explains everything about how Bear justifies what he does later.
2. It was so good that I didn't even think Bear even connected the dots that Ian wasn't his friend after the reveal. His relationship with Bear felt off from the get-go with the bad advice that the heartfelt talk was cringe (even though the waitress was really into it), to call her freaky Nikki, to refusing to play buffer at trivia night, and immediately trying to frame Bear and Nikki as a bad look (which to the viewers, very much so, but as a best friend would you have clocked it that fast, especially when you knew he was really into her?). When it was revealed that he was actually hooking up with Nikki, all those actions made a lot of sense suddenly.
  - I also think it had to do with Sarah being into Bear. Ian and Sarah would often drive around together, which makes me think she probably told Ian about being into Bear, so Ian has a full scope of the situation.

Additionally, Ian probably felt guilty that it might hurt Bear to find out they were hooking up, thus ruining the group dynamic. Selfish motives for sure, but I think putting youself in that post highschool early 20s mindset where you don't want long standing relationships to be ruined often occurs.

I was team Sarah from the jump, but we wouldn't have entertaing conflict if everyone acted on common sense
3. After learning what we do about him and Nikki on/off again hooking up, it made me mentally comb over every interaction he had with Bear. I'm not sure if all of it was self-serving, but I definitely think he was sabotaging Bear with the "Freaky Nikki" suggestion.
  - Yeah. I read the “that’s cringey” as genuine stupidity and the “call her freaky Nikki” as Deliberate sabotage. 
4. The entire movie happened because all of the main characters have such poor communication skills lol. Nobody was really able to talk about how they really felt until it was too late 
  - this is like half of the plotlines of most modern movies work, bad communication leading to drama
    - Not modern movies, all of literature forever lol 
5. What I like about Obsession is you can interpret any of the main characters in a variety of different ways - good and bad. Everyone online seems to have a different opinion whether talking about Ian, Sarah, Nicky or Bear. 

I like that. Their intentions and morality are all ambiguous and open to interpretation.
6. Were Ian actually a good friend, none of this would have happened. The heartfelt confession would have actually given Bear more clarity.

## 2. Vigo Mortensen, not Rami Malek, should have won the Oscar in 2018

**Score:** 747

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I doubt Viggo cares too much about Oscars.
  - Or even knows about second breakfast.
    - What about elevensee’s?
2. Viggo Mortensen should have won the Oscar

Which year? For which movie? How many Oscars?

All of them 
3. A much better actor in general
4. ![gif](giphy|NEvPzZ8bd1V4Y)
5. Wish someone told me how good Viggo was in Green Book sooner, only just saw it last year and it instantly became my second favorite role of his. such a fun one, seen it a few times now.

dude ate more food than I've ever seen a character eat in a movie too. It was like Brad Pitt's snacking but Viggo was slamming chili dogs, then spaghetti, then a couple of Italian subs, chugging an orange soda, a bucket of KFC, then folding a pizza in half and eating it like a sandwich. All while chainsmoking. And that was like the midway point lol.
  - Fun little detail for Sopranos fans: the person Viggo is portraying in that film would go on to play Carmine Lupertazzi in The Sopranos.
6. Viggo prob should have won around three Oscar’s to be honest….

Eastern Promises, Captain Fantastic, Good… even Hidalgo…
  - Missing History of Violence for a possible 4th

## 3. Christopher Nolan Says ‘Odyssey’ Backlash Over Casting and More Is ‘Irrelevant’ and He’s Learned Not to Worry: ‘Remember, I Spent 10 Years Dealing With Batman’

**Score:** 624

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. This goes for anything, not just the cinema industry: pay for focus groups. Investing in feedback can save a fortune in any industry. 
2. So tired of hearing about this movie. 
  - I can’t wait for this movie to come out if for no other reason than people can finally have some opinions based on the actual movie itself rather than promo material and production news
3. social media is unfortunately a huge blight nowadays. it's cool to hate and try to start outrage for absolutely nothing
  - Not for nothing. To the owners, outrage means engagement which puts eyes on advertising dollars. To the instigators it means attention because they’re energy vampires. To the people engaging, to be outraged means a dopamine hit. And to the people being engaged outrage is their most visceral connection to the world so they chase it. Everyone gets something out of it. None of it is good. 
4. I’ve been on Reddit for some time now, and have begun to wonder - why do so many people have a bone to pick with Christopher Nolan in this app? Is he viewed as overrated or something?

This could also be my own tunnel vision and lack of perusing different threads concerning movies. I’m cognizant of that when posing this question.
  - Social media likes to pick a new person to tear down every month 
    - Should be top comment. There is always something to hate, and hate gets the reactions these days. 

Also it is great to see someone rise, but everyone online secretly wants to see them fall
  - He’s effectively been \*the\* modern blockbuster director for 20ish years. So if you don’t like him you’re still gonna be exposed to some degree. 

Some people in “film circles” find him overrated especially in regards to his writing (bad at writing women, plot holes). This is exacerbated by the blockbuster thing - when a bunch of people are heaping love on something you find flawed that might be irritating?

I haven’t seen a handful of his movies and haven’t rewatched anything in the past few years (sans Interstellar re-release in IMAX) but even then… his stuff is good at minimum. Mass appeal movies like blockbusters will never get full respect of a certain type of “film bro” 
5. What does that even mean? Everyone casted in Batman was great. Liam Niesom was a bit of a weird choice but outside that it was great 

## 4. Who is the best actress who has never won an Oscar?

**Score:** 318

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Glenn Close
  - And its not even close
2. Pictured ^
  - Muriel's Wedding 
3. Amy Adams
4. It's Amy Adams. 
5. Annette Bening has to be the answer here. Multiple nominations spanning decades and still no win feels criminal at this point. Her work in American Beauty showed a restraint most actors can't pull off, and 20th Century Women was equally deserving in my view. The Academy keeps snubbing her and it doesn't add up.
6. She deserves one for Hereditary. Her acting was phenomenal.
7. Amy Adams

Helena Bonham Carter

Sigourney Weaver 
  - Wow Sigourney Weaver I just assumed she’d won a supporting at least,  definitely a good shout.  The other two too but she really surprises me.  

## 5. Who Are Your Favourite Non-Human Villains in Movies? - Audrey II from Little Shop of Horrors (1986)

**Score:** 222
**Body:** I know there are several more iconic examples but I’m sticking with Audrey II (Levi Stubbs). It’s simultaneously compelling, entertaining and genuinely threatening, as it grows to be a more impressive villain (and practical effect) throughout the film.

Xenomorphs, Things, Predators and Terminators… who you got?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://preview.redd.it/uqevgl9bhnch1.png?width=450&format=png&auto=webp&s=6c9d9ceb156318d1aa0559a86f5572d2074d9b25

HAL 9000
2. Feed me Seymour!
3. ![gif](giphy|3o7TKVQksHNOSXIMzS)
4. ![gif](giphy|10wgT5PDnOwMQE)
5. ![gif](giphy|1oFtycDItaT848RjH2)
6. Watch the alternate ending for Little Shop of Horrors. It cost millions and features 100 foot tall plants taking New York (the original musical ending). And it never saw the light of day…
7. Bruce the shark in Jaws
8. ![gif](giphy|EpyBuEAUdmzzRKujxR)
9. The Graboid from Tremors franchise 
10. The T-1000

https://i.redd.it/8zhg9xvvench1.gif

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Possession (1981) the film that should not be remade…

**Score:** 64
**Body:** A benefit that it is being remade is that many more people will go and watch this extraordinary film, a film that is incomprehensible or undigestable to many kinds of audience which will see the acting as "bad" or cartoonishly exaggerated, its story as incoherent. It is in many ways a film that should not be remade in the same way that a fever-dream that you had and changed your life can't be remade, or that a traumatic event of disaster needs to be forgotten, left to the cenotaph. As I picture the Dano and Qualley coming approximation I become horrified - not that this the worst kind of IP borrowings, it isn't - because what was achieved on celluloid feels so personal, so unique and so sacrificed for, built around one of the greatest female acting performances, one of the greatest surrenderings in film history, it feels sacrilegious to touch on it with another of its kind, in what could only be a pale, pale imitation, a counterfeit risking mockery - because you cannot copy that particular kind of Zulawsky mockery, it becomes a sacrilege of a sacrilege…impossible. It feels as if the director, his camera and his actors conspired then to cast an abominable spell, a one-time-only spell to save the world (not unlike in Tarkovsky's *Sacrifice*)…and we don't know yet if it worked.

 The magic of it came out of the very particular ways that it failed…because the film is about the abject failure of communication. It crashes down on itself to prove - in the medieval torturer's sense of "prove" - its point. In many ways it was a solitary, personal kind of witch's brew spun from Isabelle Adjani's psyche and all her filmic collaborators.

 Zulowsky tried at times to re-do that spell, leaping back in time before it (The Most Important Thing: Love, etc) and after it (Szamanka, etc) among others, but the mantic sorcery could never be recast. It came out of a particular moment in time, the ounces of their souls. And if he couldn't cast the spell again why should anyone else? No one could fail in the way that he and they failed, let us not obscure what they had dared with a copy of any kind.

 I of course am writing in superlatives and absurd exaggerations….but it is all true.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ugh, did not know a remake was being done. Awful idea !

*Possession* is such a product of a particular moment: the cultural moment of Berlin before the Wall came down, and the psychological moment of Zulawski's undigested divorce trauma. Everything just happened to align at the right moment, to make the film possible. But..."you can't step in the same river twice".

It's such a risky film. Zulawski's ideas and decisions were absurd: they should not work, but somehow they do. The performances from Sam Neill and (especially) Isabelle Adjani are like watching someone on a tightrope. Very very difficult to attain that degree of edginess. And, even more difficult to replicate it.
  - >*Possession* is such a product of a particular moment...

You describe that moment really well. That film should never have worked, so it worked like no other.

When I discussed this with my wife she said: "It would be like remaking Wim Wenders' Paris, Texas (1984)", which may not seem like an obvious parallel, but it too was so personal, and so much a product of a very particular moment, and America had never really been seen like that before. Also with very individual performances. It would be like...why?
  - 100% agree! Why couldn’t we, say, put the funding behind a product, or maybe 10 products, of this particular moment, right? Remakes are such a sad symptom of the devaluation/commercialization of the arts in our culture. 
2. The only way I could be on board with the new film is if they do it *Irma Vep* style. More meta, with Adjani's role bleeding into the life of Qualley's character. 

A straight remake is like trying to catch lightning and it will most likely feel incredibly hollow unless the director happens to go through a rough divorce right before filming 
3. To me, remaking an already English language film thats only like 45 years old makes no sense. The original is perfectly watchable for the exact same audience as the remake. I just do not understand it, apart from obviously just to make money, but I also feel like it won't actually make a *ton* of money? 

If they must do it though, Paul Dano however is a good casting I must admit, but Margaret Qualley I am very very hesitant about. Unironically think Mikey Madison would be better casting, if they wanna keep it up with the current trendy actors.
  - yeah, I'm not into watching Dano do his awkward/angry act in this tbh. As much as I love Adjani's character I also don't want Sam Neill's totally bizarre, stiff, wide-eyed performance touched either. And Qualley, she is drawn to these edge roles with her own quirk. I don't know. If you wanted to go over the top Lily-Rose Depp could probably hit some notes, and feature some of the weird beauty of Adjani (she re-did Adjani already in Egger's Nosferatu). But really, just leave all of it alone.

Is this just IP money stuff where you can get funding by referencing something in existence? I'm not sure how to read this remake.

It's funny. Polish Communism ruined Zulawsky's On A Silver Globe masterpiece (tried to kill it), and Hollywood Investment Capitalism seems to be trying to ruin his most successful film. He's being hit from both sides of history.
4. They’re remaking it?!? Ugh. No! It would be so much more interesting if the people behind the remake used Possession as the inspiration for a brand new unhinged exploration of relationships. We live in a time that throws unique barriers to intimacy and communication before us, this constant technological filter between people. Why not create a new original hellscape for our era? 
  - Check out *The Untamed (2016)*
5. I cannot wrap my head around the arrogance, entitlement, selfishness, greed, and desperation of wanting to remake A PERSON’S AUTOBIOGRAPHICAL DIVORCE STORY, let alone Americanizing it when a strong part of the story is the specific geopolitical emotional context, written by a person displaced from their homeland.  
Also do we seriously think that Robert Pattinson even understands this movie? Like beyond camera angles and scream sessions? What does he feel in his day to day life that he can take on the personification of psyche archetypes? That’s not part of his day to day culture or lived experience. I guarantee he is not a person feeling two sisters in his body of Faith and Chance who are having a fight to control him. 

I almost think tapping into this thing that has so many people’s body fluids in it is apt to awaken an actual monster, just let’s hope that it goes straight for Robert Pattinson. 
  - Well, he did spend many years playing a sexy undead thing. (ok, I'm joking).

You're pretty rough on Robert here, but your points about Zulawsky and how personal this film is are very well taken. 

Maybe the director of Smile (2022) and Smile 2 (2024) will bring the life experience necessary?

## 2. If you have a chance to see Cielo (2025) in theaters, take it.

**Score:** 10
**Body:** This film was a masterclass in my opinion. The themes, the cinematography, the color-grading, the acting, the amazing landscapes, and the characters: all splendid. This movie wasn't even on my radar, but a local indie theater was playing it and I decided to check it out, and wow, I'm glad I did. I don't want to give too much away, but it's a magical realism film set in Bolivia. It definitely doesn't spoon-feed you the meaning behind everything you're seeing on screen, but it's also not too out there for the most part. It's one of those films that will affect some people deeply, depending on what you've gone through in life, while others will be confused by what they just watched.

I couldn't find a single reddit thread about this movie, so it seems like it's pretty under the radar. One of my favorites of the year so far, just a wonderfully beautiful film on so many levels. Highly recommend. I'm interested to hear from others if you've seen it, what did you think? 

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. Inside the Yellow Cocoon Shell

**Score:** 5
**Body:** I rewatched this film recently was wondering if anyone else had any thoughts on it.

It's one of those films that I enjoyed watching but struggle to say much about. I don't think it's hard to pick out themes in it, but what it's saying about them is more obscure. I could ramble a bit about it but I'll concentrate on a couple of things that stood out to me.

It's a surprisingly *noisy* film. Not just in the city, but the countryside too, mostly because of the rain and the wind. Even a quiet conversation often has a window frame rattling in the background, to a point where the noise starts to feel oppressive. The - all too brief - fade out of the sound at the end of the film is a relief.

With my materialist eye, it's easy to see the main character Thien's malaise as down-to-earth just as much as it might be spiritual. He spends much of his time uncomplainingly doing things for other and his rest and relaxation keep getting interrupted. In one of the few funny moments in the film, Thien describes waking up regularly and hearing a distorted voice coming from inside him. Then he undercuts this by saying a doctor told him it was chronic fatigue syndrome.

The close of the film, Thien lying in a shallow river, has obvious religious symbolism, of baptism and the washing away of sins. But also has a simpler reading as a time when he puts down his burdens for a bit and takes some time for himself.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. Casual Discussion Thread (July 11, 2026)

**Score:** 3
**Body:** General Discussion threads threads are meant for more casual chat; a  place to break most of the frontpage rules. Feel free to ask for  recommendations, lists, homework help; plug your site or video essay;  discuss tv here, or any such thing.

**There is no 180-character minimum for top-level comments in this thread.**

Sincerely,

David

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. Thoughts on The Invite (2026)

**Score:** 3
**Body:** Just walked out of The Invite and I thought it was a lovely, joyful, pleasant and refreshing movie about four characters in one room with razor sharp dialogue that was endlessly absorbing and brilliantly witty. 

I haven’t laughed this much in a movie theater in years. Not only is it hilarious but the drama is thematically multilayered in its psychological depth enriched by excellent performances and perfect chemistry between the actors. 

Olivia Wilde’s direction style was confident and beautifully simple with cinematography that was visually astonishing. Loved the editing of that opening scene.

I loved this little gem of a movie and it is absolutely one of the my favorites of the year. Similar to The Drama (2026) I think It’s another modern masterpiece.

What do you think?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I loved most of the movie but was a little disappointed over the last 15 or so minutes. The film constantly changes tone (in a good way) but it is mostly funny. Then, after Rogen's character (Joe) trips and hurts his back, everything turns grim, just to deliver a slight promise of a silver lining during the last minutes. And the characters, who were beginning to create an amicable understanding to each other, suddenly lash aggressively at everybody else (Joe was grumpy, then kind of gets a cheerful enlightment only to revert to being grumpy was my least favourite). It was just too drastic a change over too little time. And I would've preferred to have the overall sentiment of a funny movie as an aftertaste, to be honest. Penélope Cruz did a very good job in this film, though.
  - I think that's the litmus test. You found that ending hopeful. I found it to be the end of the relationship, or worse, they keep living in their misery. 
    - I also thought of the possibility of that being a farewell; kind of a "let's remember what good we had one last time". But anyway It was a disappointing finale for me.

---
